04 Step 2 Brainstorming Metrics per Team or Function - YouTube.mp4
The next step in building out your company scorecard is to brainstorm the key metrics that each team is going to track and be held accountable to. Now, before we jump into metrics, metric and KPI selection, there are two things I need to point out. First, there's a category at the top of your company scorecard template for Northstar metrics. Please leave this section blank for now because we're going to be filling it in when we go through the quarterly sprint planning process. Which is going to be covered in a future module. Second, your goal should be to select three, but ideally no more than five metrics per team. Now, at first glance, that won't seem like enough. And some of your managers may even panic a bit when you tell them that they're limited to just three key performance indicators for their entire team.
But... Here's what you need to keep in mind. Each team should still have their own team-based scorecards in addition to the overarching company scorecard that we're building here. So for example, your product team will have a scorecard. Your marketing team will have a scorecard. Your sales team will have a scorecard. You get the idea. So when it comes to selecting the metrics, that we're gonna be tracking on the company scorecard, you're simply asking your team leaders to select the three to five key performance indicators that are most important and relevant right now. Three to five metrics that tell the clearest story about how the team is actually helping the company hit its goals and how they're already tracking towards those goals. Now, sometimes the team metrics are super obvious.
You know what they should be. They know what they should be. Everybody knows the metrics that matter the most right now. But other times it isn't so obvious. And for some teams, it can be downright frustrating to narrow things down to just three to five metrics. So in those cases, here's a simple little exercise you can go through with your team to help them narrow the scope. I call it the beach vacation question. For this exercise, you... and your team members imagine the following scenario. I want you to imagine that you and the people that you love most are enjoying an amazing beach vacation. You've already been gone a week, but you're having such a great time that you're thinking about extending the trip for an extra week.
And best of all, the resort has just informed you that if you do decide to go ahead and extend, you can do so for half price. Pretty cool, right? So it's a no-brainer from a cost perspective. So the only question is: can you personally be away that long? So here's the question that you and your team must answer. What three to five metrics would you need to see to know beyond the shadow of a doubt that it's safe to extend your vacation? Vacation an extra week or that you need to catch the next flight home. Now I have found this scenario to be helpful in narrowing the scope down to the metrics that truly matter versus the ones that are just kind of your feel-good vanity metrics.
For product and engineering teams, it can be metrics such as weekly or monthly active users, activation rate, milestone achievement, unit churn, active defects, commit to deploy times, and net promoter scores. For marketing teams, it could be metrics such as unique visitors, cost per acquisition, return on ad spend, total qualified leads, list revenue, and average customer value. Just name a few. For sales teams, you might look at sales accepted leads, total qualification calls, total close... closing calls, close rate, booked revenue, quota, attainment per rep, remaining pipeline, average deal size, you get the idea. If you have a customer success or customer support team, you might look at... metrics such as total support tickets, average response time, number of clients onboarded, renewal rate, expansion revenue, and net promoter score.
And for your finance and accounting team, you might look at metrics like monthly or annual recurring revenue, budget variance, gross margin, net profit margin, current ratio, burn rate. Or 90-day receivables, again, you get the idea. Now, just to be clear, there's nothing magical about the metrics I just gave you. These are simply examples of metrics that we and... metrics that many of our clients have utilized in the past. You can actually find more examples in the metrics tab on the company scorecard template, but again, don't feel like you have to use all. or even most of the examples that we have given you. If you see a metric and think, 'Ooh, I would definitely like to see that one,' then perhaps you consider adding it to your list.
If you see a metric and think, 'Don't even know what the heck that is,' then perhaps you ignore it for now. Either way, spend some time with your team and brainstorm the three to five metrics that are most important and most relevant right now. For each and every core department in your company. These will serve as the dials on the instrument panel that you will use to navigate your business from where you are now to where you ultimately choose to go, even when the weather turns rough. So give it some thought, do some brainstorming, talk to your team, and once you're ready, you can move on to the next step.
